_doc: Editor steering notes for the conference preview agent. Authoritative — these override prior knowledge and any conflicting context-data interpretation. Keep each note to 1–3 short sentences. Empty strings are dropped silently. Loaded by build_conference_context.py from /cfb-research/writer_notes/<season>/<conf>.json.
_usage: conference_note: 1–3 sentences with two purposes — (a) the meta-narrative for the year (e.g., 'The realignment dust has settled but the talent gap between the top three and the rest is still widening.'), and (b) calibration the agent needs to right-size confidence: what 'good' looks like in this league. SEC example: 'In modern SEC: 7-1 in conf typically reaches the title game; 9-0 has never happened. 9 overall wins is a historic season.' MAC example: 'In the MAC, 8 conference wins is championship caliber; 6-2 contends.' Combine narrative + calibration when both matter.

team_notes.<slug>: 1–3 sentences about that specific team — what the data won't tell the agent (a coaching change, a critical transfer, an injury, an off-field story, the angle the writeup should take).
Slugs match the canonical team slugs in build_team_context.py / scrape_team_context.py.
conference_note: The Big 12 is a P4 conference but trails the Big Ten and SEC in talent and funding — its CFP candidates almost always come through as the conference's auto-bid champion, not as at-large dark horses. Three storylines define the 2026 Big 12: (1) Texas Tech is the preseason favorite IF QB Brendan Sorsby plays — Tech won the 2025 conference title and made the CFP (lost to Oregon, offense limited), but Sorsby (portal addition from Cincinnati) entered gambling rehab and is likely facing NCAA suspension; if he misses the season, Tech's 'clear favorite' status is fragile and a half-dozen contenders are ready to fill the vacuum. (2) Oklahoma State bounce-back — new HC Eric Morris brought a critical mass of his North Texas players with him via the portal; expect a meaningfully different team than the dismal 2025 version. (3) Two of the league's top players will likely miss the season for off-field reasons — Sorsby (above) and BYU WR Parker Kingston, who is facing serious criminal charges; the agent should write the Kingston situation with formal/legal language and NOT speculate beyond what has been publicly reported. Volatility calibration: the Big 12 has the loosest hierarchy in the P4 — 16 teams playing a 9-game conference schedule means nobody plays a clean cross-section, and recent results show multiple different champions and a preseason favorite that misses the title game more often than not. When team_notes give a 3-tier range (e.g., Tier 2-4), that's typical for this league, not a hedge. Frame the wide projections as the story: this is a league where the August #8 finishes 2nd in December more often than anywhere else. Resist flattening 3-tier ranges into a single outcome; the uncertainty IS the projection. Big 12 tier framework (tiers describe team caliber, NOT exact record): Tier 1 = Big 12 title-game favorite if healthy and CFP-bound (typically as the conference auto-bid, not an at-large); Tier 2 = title-game contender, dark-horse CFP at-large; Tier 3 = middle tier and title-game outsider, in the contention mix but outside CFP; Tier 4 = rebuilding year, fighting to get to a bowl game; Tier 5 = down year, where reaching bowl eligibility would itself be the ceiling. When team_notes give a range, the agent should treat the wider boundary as the realistic downside — but per the volatility note above, 3-tier ranges are common here and the range itself should be framed as the projection rather than collapsing to a single tier.
